questions.text: My baseline emotional state is one of calm readiness.
I find that I am better at identifying what someone actually needs when I set aside the emotional coloring of their situation.
I step aside willingly to give others opportunities to lead, even if I feel capable.
When I feel inspired, the energy is so strong that sitting still feels impossible.
I find that my emotional responses don't produce noticeable physical symptoms.
I notice that physical sensations rarely accompany my emotional states.
I feel that personal reading time is a non-negotiable part of my schedule.
I often need time alone after social events to process the intensity of my reactions.
I feel that the obligation to respond to pain is a commitment that defines the core of my ethical life.
I routinely bring extra food, supplies, or materials to share with whoever might need them.
I hold the view that a detached, methodical engagement with human suffering is the posture that most effectively combines clarity of thought with depth of commitment.
I hold the view that it is perfectly acceptable to choose personal benefit over collective benefit.
I find that I miss the social meaning behind the order in which people are greeted or acknowledged.
I find myself noticing the cadence and rhythm of a conversation, not just the words.
I routinely accept inconvenience so that other people's lives run more smoothly.
I notice that my pulse quickens noticeably in response to emotional stimuli.
I believe that the moral weight of someone's suffering should never be discounted simply because responding to it is inconvenient.
I believe that my personal brand—how I am perceived—is something I should actively manage.
I tend to miss the political subtext in professional communications.
I feel empowered when I can make executive decisions.
I am aware of the invisible alliances that form in any group setting.
I actively lobby for my perspective before a group makes a decision.
I keep a running list of things I need to remember and review it regularly.
I quickly establish the pecking order in any new group I join.
I would choose a less prestigious school or job if the alternative let me stay close to people who need me.
I do not usually notice when a text message carries emotional subtext that goes beyond the words.
I wait for the right mood to tackle unpleasant tasks, knowing the timing matters.
I believe that the best creative work happens when all structure is removed.
In high-pressure situations, I look to collaborate and distribute the load.
I complete tasks well before their deadlines whenever possible.
I hold the view that detached, methodical reasoning about painful situations is the approach that yields the broadest view and the most thoughtful response.
I am content to contribute to something larger without needing personal recognition.
I hold the view that speed in execution is what keeps an idea alive long enough to matter.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that gives my sense of justice its emotional foundation and its motivating power.
I notice when someone offers a half-truth that is technically accurate but misleading in context.
I verify that backups of important data are running correctly on a regular basis.
